usage_note: Orders are differentiated by priority, size, product mix, and customer class — multiple entity classes flow through the same picking process with different service time distributions and routing rules.
Retail vs. wholesale customers create distinct order classes with different SLAs and throughput priorities.

Order arrival rates vary heavily by time of day (post-10-AM spike for standard orders, late-afternoon spike for same-day commitments) and day of week (Mondays and Fridays typically heavier). Peak-season volumes can exceed baseline by 3-5x.
Same-day orders carry priority attributes that jump them ahead of standard orders in the picking queue.
In batch picking variants, orders are consolidated into batches before being released to pickers — the batch becomes the unit of arrival at the picking workstation.
In multi-picker zones, orders can be routed to the picker with the shortest current queue (shortest-job-first or JSQ at the zone level).

Routing decisions are highly strategy-specific — the same blueprint supports piece/batch/zone/wave/goods-to-person routing based on the configured strategy. Strategy selection is itself a conditional routing decision at the WMS level.
Zone picking uses parallel zone-level pick streams that merge at a downstream consolidation point. Wave picking adds time-based sequencing to the parallel zone structure.
Pickers operate on shift-based schedules — the Seeder workflow needs to represent shift availability, breaks, and handoffs.
Pickers are shared across zones in some strategies (cross-trained pickers moving between zones as needed). Packing stations and dock doors are shared downstream resources.
Same-day orders jump ahead of standard orders in the order backlog queue — priority-based service discipline rather than FIFO.
